content:
We index the set M = {1,2,4,5,7,8,…} by m↦aₘ, where
 if m=2q−1 (odd) then aₘ=3(q−1)+1,  
 if m=2q   (even) then aₘ=3(q−1)+2.  

We seek 2n consecutive aₖ, …, aₖ₊₂ₙ₋₁ whose sum is 300.  Pairing them two‐by‐two gives two cases:

Case I: k=2q₀−1 is odd.  Then the 2n terms split into n odd‐even pairs
  (2q₀−1,2q₀), (2q₀+1,2q₀+2),…, each pair summing to 6(q₀+i)−3.  
  Hence
    S = ∑_{i=0}^{n−1}[6(q₀+i)−3]
      = 3n(2q₀ + n −2)
    = 300
  ⇒ n(2q₀+n−2)=100
  ⇒ q₀ = (100 −n²+2n)/(2n).  

Case II: k=2q is even.  Now the pairs are (2q,2q+1),(2q+2,2q+3),… each summing to 6(q+i).  Thus
    S = ∑_{i=0}^{n−1}6(q+i)
      = 3n(2q + n −1)
    = 300
  ⇒ n(2q+n−1)=100
  ⇒ q = (100 −n²+n)/(2n).

Checking n=1,…,10 for integral q≥1 one finds solutions
  Case I: n=2 (q₀=25), n=10 (q₀=1)
  Case II: n=1 (q=50), n=4 (q=11), n=5 (q=8).

Hence the possible n are

\boxed{1,2,4,5,10}.

 if m=2q−1 (odd) then aₘ=3(q−1)+1,

      = 3n(2q₀ + n −2)

Case II: k=2q is even.  Now the pairs are (2q,2q+1),(2q+2,2q+3),… each summing to 6(q+i).  Thus
